title: Strongly-coupled non-Markovian waveguide QED with input-output HEOM
authors: Neill Lambert, Yi-Te Huang, Yueh-Nan Chen, Paul Menczel, Franco Nori
abstract: We consider the problem of modeling a single qubit in contact with a one-dimensional waveguide beyond the standard perturbative and Markovian approximations. Using the recently developed input-output hierarchical equations of motion (io-HEOM), we investigate multiple examples of such waveguides, characterized by different spectral densities. Our examples highlight that the io-HEOM method can accurately capture non-Markovianity in waveguide QED from two distinct origins. The first source of non-Markovianity is spatially non-local coupling between the qubit and the waveguide. By examining two examples with non-local coupling, we show how the coupling function affects the steady-state bound photons, and demonstrate the release of these photons when the qubit energy is quenched. The second source of non-Markovianity is non-linear dispersion. We illustrate this scenario using the example of a cavity array with point-like coupling, where the non-linear dispersion leads to persistent oscillations due to Van Hove singularities in the spectral density.
summary: This paper introduces the 'input-output Hierarchical Equations of Motion' (io-HEOM) to simulate strong-coupling dynamics in waveguide QED. It demonstrates how the method captures non-Markovian effects from both spatial non-locality and non-linear dispersion. The work is significant for its ability to model the dynamics of virtual photon release and persistent oscillations in structured environments.
main_problem: Modeling a single qubit in a 1D waveguide beyond the standard Markovian and perturbative approximations, specifically addressing non-Markovianity arising from non-local coupling and non-linear dispersion.
main_result: The developed io-HEOM method accurately captures non-Markovian dynamics, including the release of virtual bound photons during a qubit frequency quench and persistent oscillations due to Van Hove singularities in cavity arrays.
method: The input-output Hierarchical Equations of Motion (io-HEOM) framework, supplemented by the Prony method for correlation function fitting and Silbey-Harris polaron theory for analytical validation.
model_or_system: A single qubit interacting with a 1D waveguide, including both continuous waveguides with linear dispersion and 1D cavity arrays with non-linear dispersion.
key_observables: Photon density/occupation, qubit population, spatial photon profile, and renormalized qubit frequency.
important_parameters: Strong-coupling and ultra-strong-coupling regimes, spectral density (Ohmic and Lorentzian), and non-local coupling functions.
main_assumptions: The multi-exponential ansatz for the bath correlation function is truncated at a finite hierarchy level, and the Prony method fits the correlation function only up to a fixed finite time.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic of the two studied waveguide scenarios and the io-HEOM framework; Figure 2 shows photon density, qubit population, and steady-state profiles for an Ohmic waveguide; Figure 3 illustrates photon emission following a frequency quench; Figure 4 shows dynamics for a Lorentzian spectral density; Figure 5 demonstrates the Prony method for correlation function fitting; Figure 6 shows photon occupation and qubit dynamics in a cavity array.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the io-HEOM framework, applies it to a continuous waveguide with non-local coupling to study bound photons and photon release, extends the study to cavity arrays with non-linear dispersion, validates results using polaron theory, and discusses the numerical implementation via the Prony method.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful, unified numerical tool (io-HEOM) for studying non-Markovian waveguide QED in regimes where standard approximations fail, specifically addressing complex phenomena like photon-atom hybridization and Van Hove singularities.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. In this work we study two examples of non- Markovian waveguide QED: a qubit coupled either to a con- tinous waveguide with non-local coupling (the blue shaded region) and linear dispersion (top left), or to a cavity ar- ray with local coupling (blue line) and non-linear dispersion (top right). By casting both cases into a standard open quantum systems framework, via the spectral density J(ω), we can treat these cases with the hierarchical equations of motion. To recover how the waveguide photon occupation nx(t) (yellow shaded region in the upper left figure, yellow crosses in the upper right figure) responds due to the interac- tion with the qubit we extend the recently developed...

Figure 2. Dynamics and steady state in Example 1, an Ohmic waveguide with linear dispersion and exponential cut- off. (a) We show the photon density nx(t)/∆x as a function of position xωs/c, offset on the y-axis to represent different times tωs. The lines are colored to make them easier to distinguish visually. The spatial extent of the waveguide is in principle infinite; we only show a finite region here. We use the Ohmic spectral density Eq. (19) with coupling strength λ = 0.39, exponential cutoff ωc = 2ωs, and zero temperature (T = 0). The initial condition is the waveguide in the vacuum and the qubit, at position x = 0, excited. We see two main features: a propagating wave front, moving...

Figure 3. We demonstrate how a quench of the qubit frequency can lead to emission of photons out of the (virtual) bound state, for the case when a qubit is coupled to an Ohmic waveguide with exponential cut-off and linear dispersion. (a) Photon density nx(t)/∆x as a function of position xωs/c, offset on the y-axis to represent different times tωs. The color of the curves distinguishes pre-quench (black) and post-quench (red) times. (b) The green curve shows the spectral density used in this example, an Ohmic spectral density with the same parameters as in Fig. 2. The vertical black line shows the qubit frequency pre-quench, and the vertical red line shows the frequency post-quench. As...

Figure 4. Dynamics and steady state for Example 2, a qubit coupled to waveguide with Lorentzian-like spectral den- sity (due to very non-local coupling), and linear dispersion. (a) Photon density nx(t)/∆x as a function of position xωs/c, offset on the y-axis to represent different times tωs. We use the underdamped spectral density Eq. (29) with coupling strength λ = 0.4ω3/2 s , width Γ = 0.1ωs, resonance frequency ω0 = ωs, and zero temperature (T = 0). The initial condition is the waveguide in the vacuum and the qubit, at position x = 0, excited. Figure 5. Correlation Function Fitting for Example 3, a cavity-array waveguide. (a) We use a Prony method to fit the bath correlation function Eq. (37). The plot shows the real (“R”) and imaginary (“I”) parts of the original correlation function (solid curves) and of the the Prony fit result using seven exponentials (dashed curves). Note that the imaginary parts have been off-set downwards by 0.01/Ω2 for visual clar- ity. The black solid line represents the long-time envelope of Eq. (37), which is ∝g2 0/√πgct.
